resume:
Aisha Bello
Software Engineer (Frontend) | ~2 years experience

Lumen Health (B2B SaaS) — Software Engineer (2023 – present)
- Build and maintain features in a React/TypeScript web app used by clinic staff
- Led a frontend performance effort that improved the initial load experience
- Reduced flaky tests in the CI pipeline and improved build reliability
- Collaborated with design and backend on a new scheduling module

Codeworks Bootcamp — 2022

Skills: React, TypeScript, Jest, Webpack/Vite, REST, Git.
Note: accomplishments described qualitatively; no load times, percentages, or counts given. Education: B.S. Biology.
jd: Lumen Health is hiring a Frontend Software Engineer (React/TypeScript) for our clinical scheduling product. You'll build user-facing features, improve performance and reliability, and work closely with design and backend. We care about engineers who can quantify their impact — load-time deltas, bundle-size reductions, test-flakiness rates — and explain the mechanism behind an improvement, not just that it 'felt faster.' Requirements: 1–3 years building production React apps, solid TypeScript, testing experience (Jest/RTL), and the ability to profile and reason about frontend performance. Healthcare or regulated-domain experience a plus.

history.q: Give me a quick overview of what you work on at Lumen Health.
Let's talk about the frontend performance effort. What was slow to begin with?
How did you confirm that was the bottleneck — what tooling did you use?
Walk me through the first change you made.
Was it a code-splitting change, a rendering change, or something on the network side?
Did you change anything about how data was fetched on that screen?
How did you make sure the change didn't regress correctness?
Who reviewed it, and was there any pushback on the approach?
Did the change ship to all users at once or behind a flag?
How did you monitor it after release?
Separately — the flaky-test work. What was making tests flaky?
What was your fix for the flakiness?
Back to performance: what did the initial load look like before your work, in seconds?
And after — what did it become?
How are you measuring 'initial load' exactly — first paint, time-to-interactive?
So put a number on it for me: what was the before-and-after on that load metric?

candidate_last_answer: So the situation was the clinic-staff dashboard felt sluggish on first load, and a couple of users mentioned it. My task was to make the initial load feel faster. What I did was profile it in Chrome DevTools, I saw we were shipping one huge JS bundle and rendering a big list eagerly, so I introduced route-based code-splitting and lazy-loaded the heavy charting library, and I virtualized the patient list so we weren't mounting hundreds of rows up front. The result was it definitely felt a lot snappier afterwards — the team noticed, and the users who'd complained said it was better. I'm confident it was a real improvement, it just felt much more responsive on that first paint.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it definitely felt a lot snappier afterwards

expected-yield: the missing quantified Result — the before/after on a concrete load metric (TTI or first contentful paint, in seconds or ms) and the bundle-size delta, captured from a tool rather than from how it 'felt'
